plainMeaning: We bow to the Jinas, the gods of gods, from whom all quarrel, impurity, attachment, hatred, and rebirth have completely vanished.
recitation: vavagaya kali kalusāṇaṁ, vavagaya niddhanta rāga dosāṇaṁ | vavagaya puṇa-bbhavāṇaṁ, namotthu devāhi-devāṇaṁ ||

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Skt. vyapagata (vi-apa-√gam, 'to go off, depart')
wordByWord.notes: Past participle used adjectivally; it heads each of the three parallel epithets in this verse, so it recurs three times.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt./Pkt. kali ('contention, dispute'); here NOT 'Kali-yuga' but 'quarrelsomeness'
wordByWord.notes: Forms a dvandva with the following kalusa within the genitive-plural epithet.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. kalusa < Skt. kaluṣa ('turbid, foul, defiled'); genitive plural -āṇaṁ (< Skt. -ānām)
wordByWord.notes: The case ending carries the whole compound vavagaya-kali-kalusa and agrees with devāṇaṁ.
Here paired with niddhanta to intensify the sense of total eradication.

wordByWord.gloss: burnt out, smelted, refined away (as dross from metal)
wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. < Skt. nirdhmāta / niṣ-√dham ('to blow away, smelt by blowing the fire')
wordByWord.notes: A metallurgical image: the passions are not merely absent but 'smelted out' like impurities purged from ore.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. rāga (√rañj, 'to be coloured, dyed, impassioned')
wordByWord.notes: First of the binding pair rāga-dveṣa.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. dosa < Skt. dveṣa ('hatred, aversion'); genitive plural -āṇaṁ
wordByWord.notes: Closes the rāga-dosa pair; ending again agrees with devāṇaṁ.
Heads the final, climactic epithet — the end of rebirth itself.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. puṇa < Skt. punar ('again, anew'); sandhi-joined to the next word
wordByWord.notes: Combines with bbhava to form punabbhava, 're-becoming.'

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. bhava < Skt. bhava ('existence, becoming'; √bhū); doubled bbh- from sandhi with puṇa; genitive plural -āṇaṁ
wordByWord.notes: puṇa-bbhava = punarbhava, the cycle of rebirth; its 'departure' means liberation (mokṣa).

wordByWord.gloss: may there be homage; homage be (to)
wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. namo + tthu < Skt. namaḥ + astu ('let there be obeisance'); the same opening as the Śakra-stava 'namotthuṇaṁ'
wordByWord.notes: An optative salutation, stronger than a bare 'namo' — it wishes homage into being.

wordByWord.gloss: over the gods / above the gods (god-of-)
wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. for the Skt. element devādhi- (deva + adhi, 'above/over the gods'), first member of devādhideva
wordByWord.notes: Joins with devāṇaṁ to yield devādhideva, 'god of gods.'

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. deva < Skt. deva ('god'); genitive/dative plural -āṇaṁ
wordByWord.notes: devāhi-devāṇaṁ = 'to the gods-of-gods' — the Jinas, worshipped even by the celestials.

literalTranslation: To those from whom strife and impurity have departed; to those from whom attachment and aversion have departed, smelted away; to those from whom re-birth has departed — homage be to the gods of gods.
idiomaticTranslation: Homage be to the Gods of gods — the Jinas — from whom all quarrel and defilement have vanished, in whom desire and hatred are smelted utterly away, and for whom the wheel of rebirth has forever ceased.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This is a salutation built as a tricolon: three genitive-plural epithets, each opened by 'vavagaya' (gone), all resolving onto the single object 'devāhi-devāṇaṁ' — the gods of gods. The triad is purposefully graded. First the outward and gross are removed: 'kali' (strife) and 'kalusa' (defilement). Then the inner root-passions: 'rāga' and 'dosa,' qualified by the vivid 'niddhanta' — not merely absent but smelted out, as a smith blows away dross from molten ore. Finally the result that follows once the passions are burnt away: 'puṇa-bbhava,' rebirth itself, has departed. The verse thus traces cause to effect — purge the defilements, the passions are smelted, and rebirth ends. The optative salute 'namotthu' (let homage be), echoing the Śakra-stava's opening, then crowns the three negations with a single affirmation: these purified ones alone are worthy of the title devādhideva, revered by the very gods. Moving on from the phala-stuti just before it, the hymn now turns from stating benefits to rendering closing homage.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse compactly states the Jain causal chain of bondage and release: rāga-dveṣa (attachment and aversion) are the kaṣāya-driven roots that bind karma; their eradication ('niddhanta') is the destruction of the ghātī-karmas, and the cessation of 'punarbhava' is mokṣa — the end of saṁsāra. The title devādhideva asserts the Jain hierarchy in which a liberated, vītarāga Jina stands above all heaven-dwelling devas, who themselves remain within saṁsāra.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting this verse, the devotee names the exact obstacles to be shed — quarrel, impurity, desire, hatred — and holds up the Jina as proof that they can be utterly burnt away, not merely suppressed. It reframes worship: one bows not to a fortune-granting deity but to a model of total passionlessness to be emulated.

crossReferences.text: Namotthuṇaṁ (Śakra-stava) — 'namo'stu' salutation form and the devādhideva epithet
Tattvārtha-sūtra 10.2–3 — destruction of kṣaya of karmas and attainment of mokṣa (end of punarbhava)

sources.title: Navsmaran (Romanized, with English meanings) — Ajitaśānti Stava
sources.consultedFor: Verse text and devotional sense of the Ajitaśānti Stava; the namotthu/devādhideva salutation in context.

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon (Monier-Williams)
sources.consultedFor: Skt. roots underlying the Prakrit tokens: vyapagata, kaluṣa, nirdhmāta, dveṣa, punarbhava, devādhideva.

sources.title: Sanskrit Dictionary (Cologne-based aggregate lexicon)
sources.consultedFor: Verification of devādhideva ('god of gods') and kaluṣa ('turbid, defiled').

notes: Token segmentation: 'kali kalusāṇaṁ', 'niddhanta rāga dosāṇaṁ', and 'puṇa bbhavāṇaṁ' are read as single genitive-plural compounds (the case ending falling on the last member) that all qualify 'devāhi-devāṇaṁ'. The book's verbatim orthography is preserved, including the doubled 'બ્ભ' (bbh-) from puṇa+bhava sandhi and the split spelling 'પુણ બ્ભવાણં'. 'niddhanta' is glossed via Skt. nirdhmāta (smelted/blown-out); some lexica also relate it to nirdhānta — both yield 'purged/burnt out'. 'kali' here is the common Prakrit noun 'strife/quarrel', NOT a reference to Kali-yuga (a misreading found in some popular paraphrases). One web source erroneously rendered 'kali kalusa' as 'sins of Kali Yuga'; this is rejected. Verse-count traditions vary (46 vs 47); the book's numbering is followed.
